skill.name: network-engineering
skill.description: Guidance for network architecture, DNS, load balancing, TLS configuration, and connectivity troubleshooting.
skill.summary: Plan network designs and troubleshoot common connectivity, DNS, TLS, and performance issues.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown examples as shell execution, hardcoded IPs, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance. Most high severity items are false positives from prose or documentation examples, but the skill does provide dual-use network diagnostic and packet capture commands that require authorization.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use network reconnaissance guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill lists diagnostic commands for port checks, route tracing, DNS resolution, interface discovery, active connections, latency testing, and packet capture. These are legitimate network engineering tools, but they can expose sensitive topology data or be misused against systems without authorization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in a troubleshooting section and match the skill purpose. The risk is contextual because the file is documentation, not executable code.

medium_findings.title: External command examples require operator review
medium_findings.description: The skill provides shell examples for DNS queries, TLS checks, and connectivity diagnostics. These examples are not run automatically, but users could copy them into environments where they reveal network details or contact external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are directly visible in Markdown code blocks. They are operationally normal for network support, so the finding is moderate rather than high.

low_findings.title: Static weak-cryptography matches are false positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography hits occur in descriptive text such as the skill description, design wording, and section names. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or insecure cipher implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found normal network design language at the cited lines. There is no cryptographic code or recommendation for weak algorithms.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded network addresses are documentation examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP findings are private VPC CIDR examples, documentation-only DNS examples, and a public resolver example. No evidence found that the skill contacts these addresses or exfiltrates data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The addresses appear in architecture and DNS reference sections. They are examples in Markdown, not runtime endpoints.

low_findings.title: Filesystem pattern is limited to standard shell redirection
low_findings.description: The filesystem finding is a TLS debugging example that redirects stderr to /dev/null. No evidence found of file deletion, persistence, credential harvesting, or hard link abuse.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is a conventional openssl diagnostic command. It does not create or modify sensitive files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Packet capture command example
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes tcpdump guidance. Packet capture can collect sensitive traffic metadata or payloads and should only be used with permission.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and packet capture is inherently sensitive. The context is legitimate troubleshooting documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Port and connection enumeration examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes netstat, ss, nc, telnet, and active connection examples. These commands are normal diagnostics but can disclose exposed services and connection state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in the troubleshooting section. Their security impact depends on authorization and environment.

content.value_statement: Network teams need clear guidance for architecture, routing, DNS, TLS, and performance problems. This skill provides structured references and troubleshooting workflows for common infrastructure tasks.
actual_capabilities: Explains VPC subnet layouts for public, private, and database tiers.
Compares application, network, classic, and gateway load balancer use cases.
Lists DNS record types and practical DNS debugging workflows.
Recommends modern TLS settings and certificate troubleshooting checks.
Provides connectivity, routing, interface, and performance diagnostic steps.
limitations: Does not include executable automation or validated scripts.
Does not replace vendor documentation for cloud-specific limits.
Requires authorized access before running diagnostic commands.
Does not provide deep packet analysis or incident response playbooks.

use_cases.title: Design a cloud network layout
use_cases.description: Plan public, private, and isolated subnets with practical traffic flow guidance.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot service connectivity
use_cases.description: Work through DNS, routing, port, and application checks in a clear order.

use_cases.title: Review TLS and load balancing choices
use_cases.description: Compare load balancer types and validate certificate behavior for production services.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Network Concept
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how public, private, and database subnets should be arranged for a small production VPC.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Troubleshooting Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a step-by-step checklist for a web service that resolves in DNS but does not accept HTTPS connections.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Design Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare an application load balancer and a network load balancer for a high traffic API with strict latency goals.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Migration Runbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a DNS and TLS migration runbook for moving a production service to a new load balancer with minimal downtime.

output_examples.input: A private application server cannot reach a managed database.
output: Check instance state, route tables, and subnet placement.
Verify security group rules in both directions.
Confirm DNS resolution and application listener configuration.
Review network ACLs only after simpler controls are verified.

output_examples.input: A team needs to choose a load balancer for HTTPS traffic.
output: Use an application load balancer for HTTP routing, host rules, and path rules.
Use a network load balancer for TCP workloads, static IP needs, or very low latency.
Document health check paths, expected status codes, and failover behavior.

output_examples.input: A domain migration needs a low-risk DNS plan.
output: Lower TTL values before the migration window.
Validate target records before switching traffic.
Monitor resolution from multiple resolvers after the change.

best_practices: Run diagnostic commands only on systems and networks where you have permission.
Start troubleshooting with DNS, routing, and listener checks before changing infrastructure.
Record assumptions, expected traffic paths, and rollback steps before production changes.
anti_patterns: Do not run packet capture on networks that you are not authorized to inspect.
Do not change DNS TTLs or records without a tested rollback plan.
Do not treat example CIDR blocks or hostnames as production-ready values.

faq.question: Does this skill run network commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and command examples that a user must review before use.

faq.question: Can it help with cloud network design?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers subnet tiers, traffic flow, load balancers, DNS, and related design choices.

faq.question: Is it suitable for security-sensitive environments?
faq.answer: Yes, with review. Some diagnostic commands can reveal sensitive network details.

faq.question: Does it include vendor-specific cloud instructions?
faq.answer: It uses general cloud networking patterns and does not replace detailed vendor documentation.

faq.question: Can it troubleshoot TLS certificate issues?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes certificate checks, certificate chain review, expiration checks, and TLS version testing.

faq.question: What should users verify before using command examples?
faq.answer: Users should confirm authorization, target hostnames, maintenance windows, and any logging requirements.
